title: Theatre in context — world theatre traditions
summary: This lesson provides an in-depth guide to the IB Theatre HL Research Presentation (RP). It focuses on selecting and analysing a world theatre tradition, conducting meaningful practical exploration, and synthesising these elements into a high-scoring presentation that meets the top-band IB assessment criteria.
learningObjectives: Analyse a world theatre tradition through its cultural, social, and theoretical contexts.
Design and document a practical exploration of a specific performance convention from the chosen tradition.
Synthesise academic research and practical discoveries to create new, insightful understanding.
Structure and deliver a coherent, engaging, and critically informed 15-minute presentation.
Apply the IB assessment criteria for the Research Presentation to self-evaluate and refine work for the highest markbands.

simpleExplanation.title: From Archive to Action: Mastering the Research Presentation
simpleExplanation.summary: The Research Presentation is a solo project where you become both a theatre historian and a practitioner. You'll dive deep into a world theatre tradition, physically experiment with one of its performance conventions, and then present your journey of discovery, connecting your academic research with your practical experience.
simpleExplanation.analogy: Think of yourself as a culinary anthropologist. First, you are the researcher, studying ancient texts to understand why a culture uses a specific, rare spice (the performance convention) and the history behind the dish (the cultural context). Then, you become the chef, going into the kitchen to experiment with that spice yourself, feeling its texture and tasting its flavour (the practical exploration). Your presentation is the final 'tasting menu' where you not only serve the dish but also explain the story behind the spice, how you learned to use it, and how your practical cooking experience changed your understanding of the ancient recipes.
steps: Select & Focus: Choose a specific world theatre tradition and a single, manageable performance convention within it. Formulate a precise research question that will guide your entire project.

Investigate & Embody: Conduct thorough academic research on the tradition's context and theory. Simultaneously, engage in practical, physical explorations of your chosen convention, meticulously documenting your process and discoveries.
Synthesise & Structure: Weave your research and practical findings together. Analyse how your practical work illuminated, challenged, or expanded upon your academic understanding, and vice versa.

Present & Persuade: Deliver a polished 15-minute presentation that articulates your research journey. Use clear signposting, integrated visuals, and precise terminology to justify your conclusions and demonstrate your depth of learning.

flashcards.back: A significant performance practice with a recognised history, theoretical basis, and set of conventions, situated within a specific cultural context (e.g., Kabuki, Commedia dell'arte, Kathakali).
A specific, established technique, practice, or device used within a theatre tradition that is understood by the audience (e.g., the *mie* pose in Kabuki, the use of *lazzi* in Commedia dell'arte).
The social, historical, political, religious, and economic environment in which the theatre tradition was created and performed. Understanding this is essential for Criterion A.
The codified principles, theories, or philosophical ideas that underpin the theatre tradition. This could include written treatises like Zeami Motokiyo's 'Fūshikaden' for Noh theatre.
The physical process of engaging with a performance convention to gain embodied knowledge. This is the focus of Criterion B and is about the learning process, not a finished performance.
Understanding gained through physical experience rather than purely intellectual study. It's what you learn by 'doing' the convention.
The core of Criterion C. It's the process of integrating practical discoveries with academic research to create a new, more profound understanding that is greater than the sum of its parts.
Assesses your understanding of the chosen tradition in its cultural and theoretical context. Top marks require specific, in-depth, and analytical understanding, not just description.
Assesses the process and learning from your practical work. Top marks require a clear connection to the convention and insightful reflection on the discoveries made.

flashcards.front: Criterion C: Synthesis of Research and Practice
flashcards.back: Assesses your ability to connect your findings from Criteria A and B. Top marks are for demonstrating how research and practice mutually informed and transformed each other.
Assesses the clarity, structure, and effectiveness of your communication. Top marks require a polished, engaging, and well-supported presentation that uses terminology accurately.
The theory and practice of dramatic composition and theatrical representation. In your RP, you are acting as a dramaturg for your own research, shaping and structuring the material for an audience.

faq.q: Do I need to be an expert performer in the tradition to get a good mark?
faq.a: Absolutely not. The assessment is on your process of learning and discovery, not on the quality of your final performance. In fact, documenting your struggles and what you learned from them is often more valuable than presenting a polished but unreflective demonstration.

faq.q: What if I can't find a live practitioner to teach me the convention?
faq.a: This is a common situation. The IB expects you to be resourceful. Use high-quality video documentation, detailed written descriptions, and instructional texts as your 'teacher'. Your practical exploration then becomes about attempting to interpret and embody those instructions. You should explicitly address these limitations and your methodology in your presentation.

faq.q: How much of the presentation should be me demonstrating versus me talking?
faq.a: This is a presentation *about* your practical research, not a performance *of* it. The vast majority should be you speaking and analysing. Use very short (5-15 second) video clips or still images from your practical work as evidence to support your spoken analysis. A long, live demonstration will consume too much time and likely won't communicate your learning as effectively as a well-structured analysis.

faq.q: My research question seems too broad. How can I narrow it down?
faq.a: A great technique is to use a 'funnel' approach. Start with the Tradition (e.g., Beijing Opera), narrow to a Role Type (*dan* - female roles), narrow further to a specific Skill (the use of 'water sleeves'), and finally narrow to its Function in a specific play (e.g., 'to express grief in *The Drunken Concubine*'). This creates a focused, manageable, and deep line of inquiry.

sections.content: The Research Presentation (RP) is a unique and defining component of the HL Theatre course. It challenges you to move beyond the role of a student and become a 'researcher-practitioner'. This is not a simple book report; it is an investigation where your body and your intellect are both primary tools of discovery. Success in this task hinges on your ability to forge explicit, insightful links between the historical and cultural context of a tradition, and your own embodied understanding gained through practical work.
Deconstructing the Task: Beyond the Basics

The RP is a 15-minute solo presentation that must be delivered and recorded in a single take. Your task is to investigate a world theatre tradition you have not previously studied, select one specific performance convention from it, and explore it practically. Your presentation must then communicate your research journey, synthesising your academic findings with your practical discoveries. The choice of tradition and convention is yours, but it must be specific enough to allow for genuine depth in the time available.

items: **Specificity is Key:** A broad topic like 'Japanese Theatre' is unworkable. A strong topic is 'The role of the fan as a transformative prop in the Noh play *Hagoromo*'.
**Researcher-Practitioner:** You must embody both roles. Your research informs your practice, and your practice informs your research.

**Process over Product:** The assessment focuses on your learning journey. Your practical exploration is about experimentation and discovery, not about delivering a perfect performance of the convention.

**Cited and Supported:** All claims, both academic and practical, must be supported by evidence. This includes academic sources and documentation of your practical work (e.g., journal entries, photos, short video clips).

sections.content: Criterion A: The Theatre Tradition – Achieving Depth and Insight

To score highly in Criterion A, you must demonstrate an 'in-depth and perceptive' understanding of the tradition's context. This means going far beyond surface-level facts. You need to connect the dots. How did the political climate, social structure, or religious beliefs of the time and place shape the very aesthetics and function of the performance? Your research should provide the 'why' behind the 'what'. For example, why does Kabuki feature such dramatic, stylised movement? The answer lies in its origins as popular entertainment in the urban centres of Edo-period Japan, its relationship with the ruling samurai class, and its need to create spectacle in large, noisy theatres.

sections.question: A student is researching the stock character of 'Il Dottore' in Commedia dell'arte. How can they elevate their analysis from descriptive to 'perceptive' for Criterion A?
sections.solution:
A mid-band response might say: 'Il Dottore is a stock character from Commedia dell'arte. He is an old, pompous doctor or lawyer from Bologna who speaks in a mix of Latin and local dialect and is meant to be funny.'

A top-band, 'perceptive' analysis would state: 'The character of Il Dottore is not merely a comic figure but a sophisticated satirical tool reflecting the social tensions of Renaissance Italy. His origin in Bologna, home to one of Europe's oldest universities, immediately establishes him as a parody of the intellectual elite. The linguistic convention of his speech—a nonsensical 'macaronic' blend of Latin and vernacular—functions as a direct critique of an academic class perceived as detached and self-important. This satirical function was crucial for the travelling troupes, allowing them to connect with local, often illiterate, audiences by satirising a universal figure of authority. Therefore, Il Dottore's mask, dialect, and physical comedy are inextricably linked to the socio-intellectual context of his creation, serving as a vehicle for social commentary.'

sections.content: Use primary sources where possible. Instead of only reading what modern scholars say about a tradition, try to find translations of original theoretical texts (like Zeami's 'Fūshikaden' for Noh or Bharata Muni's 'Natya Shastra' for classical Indian dance). Quoting from and interpreting these texts demonstrates a higher level of engagement.
Criterion B: The Practical Exploration – Documenting Embodied Learning

This criterion assesses the 'application' and 'appropriateness' of your practical exploration. Your goal is to gain embodied knowledge. You must choose exercises that allow you to investigate the core principles of your chosen convention. For example, if exploring the use of the neutral mask as taught by Lecoq, your exploration should not be about creating characters, but about achieving a state of pre-performative presence and economy of movement. Crucially, you must document this process. A journal with dated entries, reflections, photos of you attempting postures, or short, unedited clips of you working through an exercise are invaluable evidence of your learning process.

items: **Focus on Process:** Show your failures and frustrations as well as your breakthroughs. Learning happens in the struggle.
**Link to Convention:** Your practical work must be a direct investigation of the performance convention identified in your research question.

**Reflect Continuously:** Your documentation should not just show *what* you did, but what you *thought* and *felt*. Ask yourself: 'What did this exercise reveal about the performer's challenge? How did it feel physically and emotionally?'

**From the Inside Out:** The aim is to understand the convention from the perspective of the performer, not the audience. What are the internal dynamics, physical demands, and psychological states required?

sections.content: Criterion C: Synthesis – The Heart of the Investigation

Synthesis is the most challenging and most heavily weighted criterion. It is not enough to present your research and then present your practical work. You must demonstrate how they are in constant dialogue. How did a specific academic article give you a new idea for a practical exercise? How did a physical discovery in your workshop force you to re-read and re-interpret a theoretical text? A top-scoring presentation will be structured around these moments of connection, where theory and practice illuminate, challenge, and transform one another.

sections.question: A student has researched the concept of *yūgen* (subtle, profound grace) in Noh theatre and practically explored the stylised walk, *hakobi*. How can they demonstrate synthesis?
sections.solution: An effective statement of synthesis in the presentation might sound like this: 'My initial reading of Zeami's treatise described *yūgen* as the highest aesthetic goal of Noh. However, this concept remained purely abstract until I began my practical exploration of *hakobi*, the sliding walk. My first attempts felt clumsy, a mere shuffling of feet. But through documented practice [show a brief clip or series of photos], I discovered that the key was not in the feet, but in maintaining a completely still and level torso, as if floating. This physical discovery was a revelation. It allowed me to embody the principle of 'stillness in motion' that my research had described. Suddenly, *yūgen* was no longer an abstract term from a 700-year-old text; it was a tangible physical state, a feeling of profound grace generated by immense core control. My practical work did not just illustrate the theory; it translated it into a kinaesthetic understanding, proving that for the Noh actor, *yūgen* is achieved not just intellectually, but muscularly.'

sections.content: Structure your entire presentation around the argument of your synthesis. Don't save it all for the end. Use signposting phrases throughout: 'This academic finding prompted me to try the following exercise...', 'The difficulty I experienced here directly challenged what I had read about...', 'This practical breakthrough allowed me to understand the theory on a much deeper level...'
Criterion D: The Presentation – A Performance of Research

Finally, your insights must be communicated effectively. This criterion assesses the structure, clarity, and engagement of your 15-minute presentation. Think of it as a piece of solo theatre about your research. It needs a clear beginning (introducing your research question), middle (developing your argument through evidence from A and B and synthesising them in C), and end (a powerful conclusion that summarises your key learning). Your use of media—images, short video clips, sound—should be purposeful and fully integrated, not a decorative afterthought. Rehearse extensively to ensure your pacing is perfect and your language is articulate and precise.
